topic: Two colleagues are both considered for the same promotion
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed with an irritating persistence, mirroring the grating sound in my own ears. I watched Sarah and Mark move through the breakroom, their easy laughter echoing off the stainless steel appliances. It was a performance, a practiced camaraderie that felt utterly undeserved. When the announcement finally came, detailing the promotion to Senior Manager, a cold, heavy weight settled in my stomach. I kept my gaze fixed on my lukewarm coffee, the ceramic mug suddenly feeling like a lead weight in my hand. I didn't bother to look up when my supervisor approached, the pleasant tone of his voice washing over me like lukewarm water. Every syllable sounded rehearsed, every compliment a calculated gesture. I simply nodded, a small, tight movement, and retreated to my desk, the silence of my cubicle suddenly vast and suffocating.

[story 2]

My jaw tightened so hard I could feel the muscle twitching beneath my skin. I traced the grain of the mahogany desk with my fingertip, a repetitive, frantic motion. Seeing the internal memo highlighting Mark’s suitability, juxtaposed with the glossy photo of Sarah smiling beside the executive suite, felt like a physical assault. I stood up abruptly, the chair scraping loudly against the linoleum floor, a sharp noise that drew unwanted attention. "Congratulations," I managed, the words tasting like ash in my mouth. My voice sounded thin, reedy. I didn't wait for a response, just turned and walked toward the window, staring out at the indifferent cityscape, wishing the glass would just crack and let the whole unpleasant scene shatter.

[story 3]

I meticulously organized the files on my desk, aligning every folder with surgical precision, a desperate attempt to impose order on the chaos swirling inside me. Every time I glanced toward the office hallway, I caught glimpses of the competition—Sarah’s confident stride, Mark’s easy rapport. A sourness rose in my throat, a deep, unpleasant churning that made swallowing difficult. When the decision was finalized, I offered a smile that felt brittle, stretched too thin across my face. "That's wonderful news for both of you," I said, my tone deliberately flat, devoid of any genuine warmth. I focused intensely on the texture of my keyboard, willing myself to become invisible, a ghost in the machinery, utterly separate from the celebratory atmosphere they were clearly basking in.
